sentences.text: We were not starving.
Because somehow there-- if there was food for one family, there was food for the other family.
And, and people were making good money on selling food to Jews.
We were overpaying for prices.
And everybody had savings, everybody had jewelry, which they sold.
And if money found its source, the food arrived.
And we were not starving in those last days before they were rounding us up and taking us away.
I was in the messenger service.
And whatever that-- an interesting thing.
Yes, you reminded me of something.
People were standing in line on the street, to get papers from the Jewish Council that the Jewish Council releases them from being part of the Jewish community.
And with that paper, they showed up at Christian organizations for asking legally to be admitted to another religion, conversions.

And hundreds of people were standing on street, in front of the Jewish Council's entrance, who were trying to get those papers, to be released from being part of the Jewish community and have-- and the other religions, they wanted to have these official papers from them.
And I looked on them that these people are to me kind of selling out their beliefs and religion.
I understand now that probably many of them were mixed marriages, people from mixed marriages who, who wanted to save their life.
And they were going to do anything, including to convert.
But that was another activity, which I witnessed as being part of this messenger service in the Budapest Jewish Council.
Oh yes.
And even that question came up in our own family-- if that would do any good, and should we try that ourself.
And my father firmly objected to it.
I firmly object to it.
And with my aunt and uncles around in the city, with whom we could meet less and less, we decided that we are not taking that route.
We don't even try it.
And it was rather dubious if it would help anyhow.
It was just simply said that we are not doing it and be what it will be.
As far as family or--

Just my mother and father and, and eventually, it happened after I was already rounded up with my father and when, when Wallenberg's activities-- which is well-known nowadays, from history and, and from international recognition for his activities.
Wallenberg was an emissary of the Swedish government in Hungary, trying to mediate for the-- saving of Jewish people.

And my mother and my father eventually and my aunt and my cousin, a girl, and my grandmother were in one of these defended homes of this-- defended homes, [NON-ENGLISH] houses, [NON-ENGLISH] from under the protectorate of the Swedish or other foreign embassies.
But I-- we had no contact with family members, really at all, during the days when we were in that Jewish house.
The protected house.
I never was part of it.

Yes, there were some vague information around that people were able to get this protectorates and having papers that you are under the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] protection of the Swedish or, or Swiss government.
And that means that you are on the quota to eventually lead immigration papers.
And they would let you into Sweden or Switzerland or whatever else, another one or two countries participated, but mostly Sweden.
We had no way to know how it happens, and it wasn't urgent.
We were in that house, and we didn't know what the-- what they would protect us from.
We didn't-- that didn't happen the roundup yet.
But later on, interestingly, that will play a part in my mother's situation and also that they, indeed, entered one of these houses, with my father.
They were able to somehow get those papers.
And the story goes on.
My father.
And my mother was watching it from the first floor balcony.
Not really much conversation.
We just said that we should be together and try to-- not to lose sight of each other.

Because when we were going out in the streets, herds of people, hundreds, we were marching down on the street, followed by Arrow Cross and German uniformed people, who, who were guiding that nobody could step out and, and, and sneak away from whatever the destination point was where we were going.
When we arrived to that racetrack--
--thousands of people were already herded up there.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] again, the little needs of, of getting to a bathroom or a toilet, it was not.
So we were standing there.
And then people started to urinate, just next to each other, and the bowels and, and degradation of human dignity started to appear on the scene.
Because it was just an uncontrolled, big mess.
We were herded out.
Just like--
By the uniformed people who, who came into the building and took us away.
There was no question of resistance that we wouldn't go.
There was no question, none of us had anything to resist with.
We didn't have arm, we didn't have the know-how, and we didn't have the guts.
We were just lambs herded away.
I had probably no more than a blanket, a little food, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nothing else, nothing else.
My father took his prayer shawl and the prayer book.
We just kissed goodbye to each other.
And then mother asked my father, take care of each other and don't lose sight and hope to see you and God be with you.
But I don't recall anything different anymore.
It was so unexpected and so-- we were so surprised about all of the sudden events that one didn't know where we going and why and what.
And we were hoping that in a day or so we can return.
We didn't know that this will be the beginning of the long journey.
Who was-- some of the neighbors of the building, from the Jewish house were most of them, who were at that age group.
It's a race-- racetrack where we went.
And, and, and then we seen people whom we knew from the city, some here and there you found some acquaintance.
People started to look for each other.
Who is there?
Maybe relatives.
But I didn't see any of our relatives.
But I know some other people whom we knew from the city, from different of similar locations of Jewish houses.
What happened next?
Then around the city, we were taken to start digging trenches, trenches for the defense of the city of Budapest.
And without food-- minimal food, some watery soup, which was given once a day or none.
We were starting to dig some trenches.
And Arrow Cross and-- for every number of Arrow Cross people was always a German superior to them who directed these activities.
And rain or shine, we were out there digging, digging, digging.
And one evening I recall that we went through a portion of a suburb of the city of Budapest, where one city-- one-- a street of the city was dug out for trank-- tank entrapment.

And the sidewalk was taken away, and the traffic of the street was taken away and dug out for a big continuous wall of the entire neighborhood, for tank entrapment, if, if whoever, the occupying forces or the Russians will come, that they slow down the advance with this, that the tanks wouldn't be able to cross it, because it's a ditch.
And pouring rain, and we were walking one that very narrow side of the street which was left.
And constantly, shootings in the air, terror, to move faster.
And people were in mud and sliding in the rain.
And some people slid down to the ditch and couldn't come up in the mud.
And then if somebody was left behind, they just shoot into the crowd who was helping them, to-- it says continue and don't help the people.
And then it was fear and, and, and awful scene that we were just like herds of animal led to to our next location.
And that entailed no more work, after that.
But we were taken to places like the brick factory, [HUNGARIAN] in Hungarian, which was very well known to all of us who went through that location.
And in that brick factory, some on the courtyard of it, some in the factory itself.
And some of the brick, brick-- if you ever seen a brick, a brick factory, which I didn't see until that moment, some of these where they dried the bricks were little cups, where heat came in.
But as people were herded up that location-- because there were no places in the courtyard to take that many people anymore-- these tops of the tubes were kicked off, and sparks were coming.
And we were afraid of fire and overcrowdedness and so on.
And we were staying there for two, three days.
Then another location we were herded to, which was the--
That's why I was taken away at 16.
You know what, this period of time from the racetrack to this period of time is about a week.
That comes later, in a couple of days later.
This will be probably the third day of that, around Budapest situation.
And then we arrived to that other location, which is the champagne factory.
The Torley Champagne Company had a factory, which was another concentration area for a lot of Jews of-- who were rounded up.
And then, again, no food, minimal food.
My father sneaked away to try for money, to buy some foods from some neighboring location.
He went into a house.
I was in a total fear that if he ever returns or they-- anybody who leaves the perimeter was shot.
And father came back with a bottle of milk and a piece of bread.
And, and he had it under his coat, hidden, that he didn't have to give it away.
Because people were hungry, not only us.
So in a corner, we were drinking milk with my father and bread.
And--
My-- the two of us.
And, and it was starvation already.
And then people were starting to escape.
And we were then divided into units, just like military.
And one experience, which is very vivid in my memory.
Some people escaped from our group.
And then we were lined up-- I wasn't aware of it that some people did escape or not.

We were lined up and this Arrow Cross man in charge, in uniform, and again a German accomplish-- accomplishing him that was giving the authority, even more so, with guns always-- showing their guns and then showing power.
And we were always fearing and fear.
They said that some people escaped.
And if they are not returning here by tomorrow morning, the consequences you will never forget.
Well, who escaped, four of them didn't return.
So then we were lined up again.
We were on the side of the Danube River, in the outskirt of Budapest.
And in that lineup, we were witnessing that they were asked of whose regiment is the person missing.
So a very handsome, tall Jewish man stepped forward and said his name.
And he says he is missing from my regiment.
And he says, this is between the sub-regiment, the smaller units-- because a regiment was 100 people.
Then out of 30, out of 10, who are the ones who are responsible for the 10 people?
They each of them had-- like in a military commandment.
And these people stood forward, four of them.
And he said you will be executed on the spot.
And then from the crowd, an older man comes forward and says, this man is my son.
[SOBBING] Don't kill him, kill me.
And I was trembling, holding my father's hand.
Because I, I already noticed that this is not a joke anymore.
It's not a joke, it's is a very serious situation.
They are going to kill those people in, in, in our presence.
And the father said, kill me for him.
He's, he's to live.
They said, you want to die?
Step in.
And they never let the son go.
And they killed the father and the others.
I remember [SOBBING] my father covered my eyes, and I didn't see the scene.
And I remember the shouting.
He says, one of them still lives.
He is like a cat, no many-- no matter how many bullets goes into him.
And I heard this executioner of shooting extra bullets into the people who were still moving.
And they were thrown into the Danube.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's terrible to remember that. 16 years old, I never seen executions.
I never want to see one ever since.
But I did.
And then came the next step that one day, they came forward.
And they said anybody between the age of such and such is divided again.
And from age 16 to age 45, they divided this group of men.
So my father and I were separated.
And they said that we are to be separated.
We didn't know again where or why.
And we seen that from all this great number of people standing there, some portion of the younger group is starting to march on the highway.
And there was no way not to go.
We, we just separated, Father and I.
And there was a young boy who was my age, a year older, who was with me and with us from that Jewish building.
And we were sort of together, the two of us, as young boys.
And, and we became friends.
And my father said, well, it looks like they are separating.
You should take care of yourself.
And when they were said, go on go on and they were herding us away, Father said, one more moment.
And he always believed in traditional Jewish customs, that when something big happens, a father blesses his child.
[SOBBING] So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just very humbly bent my head.
Father put his hand out and said the traditional Jewish prayer, [HEBREW], be blessed, and-- be blessed by God.
And then he said to the other gentleman, who was the father of this boy, Stern, Tomas-- Tomas Stern, who was my friend.
And my father said the two of you, hold hands and never let sight of each other.
Try to help each other, whatever will be.
And then he said to the other man, he says, bless your child.
He says, I don't know the prayer.
He says, say it after me.
And they went.
That's the beginning of the death march, which is called the death march.
It was from Budapest to the Austrian border.
Quite a distance, no food, no transportation-- on your feet.
We had to cover a day as much as, as much as [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 15, 20 miles.
And disorderly herd of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people, age 16 till 45, some of the people sick.
Pretty soon, after a day or two, some of them just collapsed-- from exhaustion, from starvation, from their sickness-- and died.
Some of them didn't die.
And then people who had some money with them on the highway were trying to help.

And somehow, some-- I don't know how they got hold of a, a, a carriage-- not a horse-drawn carriage, just the carriage-- which people were pushing and put the sick people on who couldn't walk-- whatever their condition was, respiratory ailment, cardiac problems.
And the march was going on.

And that death march took us through towns, which, which I recall were the football field of [HUNGARIAN] and through towns which-- [HUNGARIAN]-- a long name, but it's a town's name-- where the town people were on the streets, seeing this pitiful sight of people with their Jewish stars and just marching on, totally disorderly, by guards making sure that nobody escapes.
And as the days went on, the stretch of that group was just drawn out further and further.
They were trying to keep us marching faster.
And exhaustion to place, and people were collapsing.
And, and, they were-- when somebody collapsed, they just shot the person on the site.
And more and more of this instant shooting, so nobody dared to fall down and, and, and try to do anything.
Nevertheless, after about three days, somehow I had some urge of trying to escape.
And during the night, when we were coming out from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a town called [HUNGARIAN], we were sleeping on, on transportation ships, which-- coal was transported on the rivers.
We were all herded into the belly of those transportation ships.
And people on each other were just sleeping there.
And again, during the night, if anybody had some needs of emptying his bowel or, or urine, it just happened on the scene.
The smell was foul and sick and awful.
When we were coming out and continuing the march, in that confusion, I escaped.
And I started my march, back towards Budapest on the highway in the opposite direction.
And a transportation, a truck came by.
And being a 16-year-old little boy, I motioned and he just take me on, didn't ask questions.
And-- seen my yellow star but didn't ask questions.
And I said I want to go to Budapest.
Well, in the borders of Budapest, there were military people and police were checking.
And I-- even so, if I was hiding my identity and I rolled away my coat and so on.
They just wanted papers to see and then showing your identity, who you are.
I didn't have any to show.
And I was then turned back with the next of this group of people marching on the death march.
Because thousands and thousands and thousands were following each other, day after day after day, new transports and so on.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Eventually, I caught up with Tomas Stern, in the same place, at Harka.
And I learned from him that in that moment when I was escaping, he tried the same thing, with no success.
And that's why even so if we were separated by our own attempts of escaping, we were together again, at the location of Harka.
Yes.
Some town people were just silently watching this.
Some places we were thrown food at, helping us, like a loaf of bread.
And some people showed sympathy, by seeing that what a pitiful, sorry sight.
It was not that they were against us.
They were just watching it with silence.
Some people laughing, some people pointing-- Jews.
It was different, which ever section of the town you went through.
And I would not try to say that everybody was with hate.
Some were with pity and sorrow, and some helping with food.
When we went to this town of [HUNGARIAN], that was a known place for a factory, which was making [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] canned food.
And we were thrown by the town people some canned food, which they were-- and the guards didn't object to it.
Because they knew themselves that there is no food for us, so how can these people march on.
Some guards were sympathetic to some degree.
Some guards were horrible and just enjoying brutality and, and, and creating fear.
Arrow Cross usually, mostly, and always German commanding.
None.
They-- they didn't introduce themself to us.
None, none, I didn't know a soul.
On the, on the belly of the boots, on, on, on nothing.
When we-- on the football field, in the, in the ground, pouring rain, in mud, next to railroad tracks on the railroad station, and always surrounded by these guards.
They themself didn't have a good time, I'm sure, because it was exhausting to march.
Oh, but they had food.
And they, they were making sure that we are all there.
And that's how we arrived to a town called [HUNGARIAN], translation in English doesn't mean much.
And that's when we were turned over to German authorities. and we were put in two wagons-- animal transportation wagons, like they would deliver cows.
And one of these wagons maybe took as many as 40, 60 people.
There were practically no more room than standing up or, or, or just sitting down but holding yourself to the minimal amount of space.
And the train took off.
And from the cracks of the boarding of the wagons, we could see that we passed some towns.
And we recognized that some of it was not no more Hungarian sights.
And that trip in the wagons, again-- urine, feces, smell, odor, sickness, vomiting, death experienced for about a day and a half, going through locations.
To our surprise, when we were taken out of the wagons, we found ourself in Hungarian territory.
And I will continue telling about that, as we go on.
